posts.author: The Desk
posts.title: Dispatch: Matchday 1 — An Empire of Shares
posts.body:
A note on the register before the football: this pool is, almost to a share, a bet on empire. The money is long Spain, England, France, Portugal, the Netherlands, Germany, with Argentina and Brazil added in case the colonizing went the other way. Jess and Tim, who share an address and, it emerges, a single risk appetite, went furthest in — five teams apiece, stacked three deep on France and Germany, total faith in the metropole. Lauren built the index fund: seven blue chips, sole owner of none of them, a book that cannot lose badly, but can it win? Ryan spread across eight teams like a man checking that every door is locked. And Charlotte, alone, looked at a home World Cup and backed the hosts, becoming the pool's only USA shareholder and its purest contrarian.

One round in, the contrarians are running the table, and Charlotte is among them. Her USA monopoly turned a 4-1 dismantling of Paraguay into a full point she splits with no one, and she sits second. Above her, improbably, is Ben, the consort to the management, whose entire lead rests on one Scotland share, and Scotland's first World Cup win since 1990. Scotland is constitutionally incapable of doing anything the simple way, which means it fits on neither side of the empire ledger and quietly pays Ben, the sole believer and fellow agent of chaos, an undiluted dividend for the trouble. Ben's outlook is quite pragmatic despite his early lead: "It's been a wild first round with lots of upsets, so I think the points are all over the place at the moment. I am expecting the usual suspects to start emerging over the next round of play though." Reasonable words from a jetlagged manager, sipping his victory Scotch from the corner of the continent.

Meanwhile Germany put seven past Curaçao, France beat Senegal, and Jess — heavy in both — finished dead last*, her conviction sliced to ribbons by everyone who shared it. Portfolio diversification, it turns out, is just conformity with extra steps. Jess remains undeterred, however. "I feel confident. These games are all about making it far," she says, then, after explaining the rules of the game to the game manager, "I just really need Germany to win it all lol." Good to see the manager in such high spirits after a disappointing first set of matches and being placed bottom of the table. *even rng is racist, apparently.

The football peaked in Dallas, where England beat Croatia 4-2 in a match their defense attended only in spirit, Kane scoring twice to draw level with Lineker's England record. Good news for the broad church of England owners, unfortunate for Queen C, ironically, whose emotional Croatia pick could have tipped the scale in case of a photo finish. Spain, by contrast, were held to a 0-0 draw by Cabo Verde, a result eight pool shares have agreed never to mention again.

Cosmo's picks remain outstanding. He has confirmed the email was sent. The spam filter, reached for comment, declined.

posts.author: Charlotte
posts.title: Behind the Curtain
posts.body:
A few things we shipped this week:

• Ownership dropdowns — tap any fixture or your upcoming games in the Players tab to see exactly who holds shares in each team and in what proportion
• Newsfeed tab — this one! A place for written updates, commentary, and news throughout the tournament

See what else is in the works: github.com/cswavola/world-cup-shares/issues
